exemplar.title: Investigating the Role of Vectors in the Catmull-Clark Algorithm for Surface Smoothing on a Pentahedron Model

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigating the Role of Vectors in the Catmull-Clark Algorithm for Surface Smoothing on a Pentahedron Model

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Wellâstructured with clear introduction, body and conclusion marked by numbered headings
Effective integration of figures, tables and flowchart to guide the reader
Appendix houses extensive derivations, keeping main text focused
weaknesses: Occasional repetition of background anecdotes increases word count unnecessarily
Minor typographical inconsistencies and layout lapses distract reader
Some superfluous quotations and long explanations detract from conciseness
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration demonstrates solid coherence and logical progression (band 3) and is exceptionally well organized (band 4), but falls short of the highest mark due to lapses in conciseness (band 3). The clear structure supports a score of 3 out of 4 in Presentation.

strengths: Highly effective use of multiple representations (GeoGebra diagrams, flow charts, Blender screenshots)
Generally correct and consistent vector notation and algebraic derivations
Logical ordering of equations with clear references and captions
weaknesses: Occasional mislabelling of figures and tables creates minor confusion
Inconsistent notation (missing boldface vectors, sign slips) in a few places
Nonstandard constant notation not always defined explicitly
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission shows mostly consistent mathematical language and notation with good logical structure (band 3), and excels in multiple representations (band 4). Minor inconsistencies limit full marks. Overall score in Mathematical Communication is 3 out of 4.

strengths: Clear personal approach demonstrated by choice of an unusual polyhedron and manual derivations
Use of Blender for verification shows thoughtful independent initiative
Firstâperson reflections add a genuine voice
weaknesses: Investigation largely follows published algorithm without novel conjectures
Approach, while imaginative, remains within conventional boundaries of subdivision studies
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evidence of independent thinking, personal approach and creativity are all at a solid level (band 2). The work is clearly engaged but does not reach outstanding innovation, meriting 2 out of 3.

strengths: Thoughtful analysis of vertexâcount growth and smoothing outcomes
Clear evaluation of computational efficiency and notes on future BÃ©zierâsurface exploration
weaknesses: Lacks deeper statistical or parameterâsensitivity analysis
Superficial comparison of Loop subdivision without discussing triangulation impact
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Reflection demonstrates meaningful insights and evaluation (band 2) with some critical analysis, but falls short of substantial, comprehensive critique required for the top band. Score: 2 out of 3.

strengths: Relevant mathematics (vector operations, weighted means) used effectively throughout
All work is commensurate with syllabus level and well applied
Derivations are coherent with supporting tables and Blender verification
weaknesses: Minor sign errors and arithmetic miscalculations appear in a few formulas
The investigation does not extend to more advanced linearâalgebra techniques (eigenâanalysis)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The mathematics is fully commensurate with course level and effectively used (band 4), with good understanding and mostly correct derivations. Minor errors preclude full marks. Final score in Use of Mathematics is 4 out of 6.

strandSelections.reasoning:
Most derivations are introduced, worked through and interpreted in a logical order; captions explain diagrams and tables; internal referencing (e.g.
âsee Table 3â) guides the reader. Minor typographical errors and one or two mis-labelled vectors reduce absolute clarity, so the standard aligns with âgood logical structure and clear communicationâ (band 3).

Derivations are largely correct and coherent; intermediate results reconcile with tables and diagrams; Blender verification supports accuracy. Minor sign errors and one duplication of forms indicate that accuracy is âmostly correctâ rather than flawless, placing understanding in band 4.

The student employs equations, 3-D diagrams from GeoGebra, colour-coded point sets, flow charts, Blender screenshots and comparison tables. These multiple representations complement each other and are always placed near the relevant text, providing a highly effective visualisation of the mathematics.

The exploration follows a clear line of thought: it opens with an introduction that motivates the choice of topic, moves methodically through each stage of the CatmullâClark algorithm, and ends with a conclusion that revisits the aims.  
â¢  Section titles (2.1, 2.2, â¦ 2.8) and logical ordering of face-, edge-, midpoint- and vertex-calculations show a coherent progression.  
â¢  Minor lapsesâoccasional repetition of background sentences and some muddled typographyâdisrupt, but do not break, the readerâs understanding. Overall coherence is good rather than exemplary, placing the work in the âcoherent and logically developedâ band.

Calculating the algorithm by hand, cross-checking with industry software and extending the study to Loop subdivision demonstrate creativity and initiative beyond a routine treatment. The work is imaginative, but not exceptionally novel or ground-breaking, meriting the âgoodâ band.

The student analyses vertex-count growth and smoothness, and evaluates the Catmull-Clark result against Blender output and Loop subdivision. While thoughtful, the analysis remains largely descriptive; deeper mathematical scrutiny of errors or parameter sensitivity is absent. Hence âthoughtfulâ (band 2) fits.

There is a clear evaluation of outcomes: correctness confirmed via software, recognition of manual-calculation limits, and comment on computational efficiency. Future implications are noted briefly. The evaluation is sound but not comprehensive enough for the top band.

The conclusion recounts findings, mentions diminishing returns after five iterations and contemplates future exploration of BÃ©zier surfaces. Reflection is woven through sections (e.g., noticing inward shift of edge points). This is meaningful but not deeply critical, corresponding to band 2.

Vector notation, over-arrows and coordinate triples are generally correct; algebraic derivations use appropriate fractional factors. A few inconsistenciesââEOABâ instead of \(\overrightarrow{OE_{AB}}\), missing boldface, and sign slipsâappear but do not impede comprehension. Hence usage is âmostly consistentâ rather than flawless.

A personal voice is present (first-person remarks, selection of Blender as a verification tool, reflection on manual effort), indicating a genuine personal approach. Yet the approach remains fairly conventional within computer-graphics explorations; thus it satisfies âclear evidence of a personal approachâ but not the highest level of innovation.

Almost all paragraphs are relevant to explaining, applying or validating the subdivision algorithm. Nevertheless the discussion occasionally digresses (e.g., extended Pixar anecdote, repeated definition of smoothness, long quotation lists) and the paper runs to 20 pages for a single-level subdivision.  
These superfluous elements prevent it from being âhighly conciseâ, yet the bulk remains purposeful, so âconcise with mostly relevant contentâ (band 3) is appropriate.

Vectors, averaging formulae and weighted means are wholly relevant to subdivision; every step hinges on these tools, showing effective use. The scope is solid but not exhaustive (e.g., no eigen-analysis or advanced linear-algebra concepts), so band 4 (ârelevant mathematics used effectivelyâ) is appropriate.

The student selects an unusual polyhedron, performs lengthy hand calculations, and later compares two subdivision schemesâevidence of significant personal initiative and independent decision-making. However, the investigation largely follows published algorithmic steps without substantial original adaptation or conjecture, so the engagement, while clear, is not outstanding.

The report is divided into an explicit introduction, main body and conclusion; numbered headings, flow-chart (Fig 1.2) and consistent page layout make navigation effortless.  
Figures and tables are integrated immediately after the text that refers to them, and an appendix houses long algebra, further improving readability.  
Because every major section is sign-posted and the internal ordering is sensible, the organization meets the description of being âexceptionally well organised with clear introduction, body and conclusion.â

All mathematics lies squarely within the HL syllabus (vector operations, averages, centroids) and is used in a sustained way. Although not pushing beyond course level, it is fully commensurate and well-applied, matching the band-4 descriptor.

annotations.comment: The repeated Pixar anecdote increases word count without adding new insight; consider summarizing its relevance more concisely.
The flow chart (Figure 1.2) effectively visualizes the aims, supporting logical progression through multiple representations.
Figure 2.2âs subfigures should match labeling in the caption; mislabeling (d) can confuse the reader.
The cover title clearly communicates the explorationâs focus and engages the reader with precise terminology.
The inclusion of personal code and page count detracts from academic content; these administrative details could be omitted.
The table of vertex coordinates lacks boldface vector notation for consistency; standardize \(\mathbf{OA}\) formatting throughout.
The initial overview paragraph clearly outlines the calculation sequence and maintains logical structure for the reader.
The general edge-point formula in Equation 6 is clearly stated, linking theory to observed inward shift.
The derivation of midpoint formula on page 9 is correct but overly detailed; consider summarizing repetitive calculations.
The link between midpoints as new vertices and their role in smoothing is insightful, reflecting clear logical structure.
The expectation statement at the end of Section 2.1 sets clear goals, reinforcing coherence between observation and planned validation.
The description âFigure 3.2â mentions Table 2 but the table number is inconsistent; verify cross-referencing for clarity.
The worked example of face-point formula is concise and correctly uses summation notation, aiding mathematical communication.
In the edge point derivation, a sign slip appears: the vector result in Equation 5 differs from the tabulated value in Equation 6.
The observation linking plotted points to inward shifts shows thoughtful interpretation of geometric results.
The table headings on page 8 repeat blank columns in Table 3; streamline header format for clearer presentation.
The explanation of new vertex formula on page 10 uses nonstandard notation for constants; define \(C_1,C_2\) explicitly with correct subscripts.
In the vertex position derivation, the final simplified formula (Equation 15) contains misaligned indices; verify subscripts for clarity.
The plotted new vertex (Figure 3.10) clearly illustrates the expected smoothing, enhancing reader comprehension.
Using Blender to verify calculations demonstrates thoughtful use of industry tools and supports accuracy claims.
The comparative table of vertex counts lacks a formal statistical analysis; include a percent change or ratio for deeper insight.
The Loop subdivision comparison lacks reflection on choice of triangulation; discuss its impact on smoothness metrics.
Assigning \(C_1=2\) and deriving \(C_2=0\) demonstrates understanding of weight influence and algorithm behavior.
The conclusion states vertex count increase but miscalculates total (5+9+7=21, not 20); correct the arithmetic for accuracy.
The discussion on diminishing returns after level 5 shows critical reflection on computational efficiency and convergence.
The derivation of new Loop edge points (Equation 12) is precise and adheres to algorithmic definitions.
The side-by-side comparison of Catmull-Clark and Loop (Table 8) is descriptive but would benefit from quantitative smoothness metrics.
The conclusion section neatly ties findings to broader applications, demonstrating meaningful reflection on algorithmic relevance.
